text: Hello friends. This young patient has subacute obstruction. So there was intestinal adhesions and CT scan has shown the thickness and the multiple bubble loop and the obstruction.

The surgeon uses Palmer's point to insert a Veress needle, introducing 2.5 liters of CO2 to insufflate the abdomen. A 10mm trocar is then inserted through Palmer's point, allowing examination of the collapsed intestinal loops.

Upon entering through Palmer's point, the surgeon observes adhesions involving the omentum and pulled-up intestinal loops. Adhesiolysis is performed using a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate the adhesions.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic intestinal grasper and a harmonic device to perform adhesiolysis on omental adhesions located on the anterior abdominal wall. The harmonic device facilitates dissection through ultrasound vibrations, creating a clear plane of separation.

The surgeon inspects the small intestine laparoscopically, identifying inter-bowel loop adhesions. The harmonic device is used to perform adhesiolysis where the omentum adheres to the bowel, while avoiding areas where two bowel serosa are adhered.

The surgeon performs sharp cold dissection using scissors near the inferior epigastric vessel, ensuring the harmonic device does not contact the vessel. The medial umbilical ligament is visible in the surgical field.

Following the completion of omental adhesiolysis, the surgeon identifies multiple bands of adhesions between the intestinal loops and the omentum. The surgeon begins to separate the omentum from the intestinal loops to address the obstruction.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic intestinal grasper and harmonic device to separate two loops of the small intestine that are adhered together, carefully pulling them apart to free them from each other.
The surgeon carefully performs cold dissection to separate serosal adhesions between the small bowel loops, avoiding the use of energy devices to preserve anatomical integrity.
in those cases farmers point entry is always better because if we'll enter through them like us you cannot have a panoramic view of the small intestine

The surgeon carefully performs adhesiolysis of the small bowel using a 30-degree telescope, rotating the light cable to visualize the bands of adhesion surrounding the bowel loops for precise cutting.
The surgeon identifies a vascular pedicle and uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate it, minimizing the risk of bleeding.
The surgeon uses a traumatic intestinal grasper with the left hand to gently hold the small intestine, while the right hand holds scissors, preparing for precise tissue manipulation.
The surgeon identifies a band of omentum near the hepatic flexure of the colon, noting its connection to the cecum. This band is visually assessed as necessary to cut.

The surgeon examines the small intestine, identifying adhesions and a bowel loop herniated into another loop. The internal herniation is addressed by releasing the adhesions and straightening the bowel loops, with a visible band holding multiple loops together.
The surgeon identifies a tight band adhering the sigmoid colon to the small bowel. Using a harmonic scalpel, the band is carefully cut to relieve the adhesion.
The surgeon checks for the presence of intestinal serosa, finding none. Entromedial traction is applied, and the harmonic device is used to cut the band, releasing the obstruction.

The surgeon separates a lump, revealing and straightening the loops of the intestine in the area. An additional omental band is dissected to prevent inter-bowel loop adhesion. The surgical field is cleared of obstructions, followed by thorough suction and lavage.

The surgeon introduces two to three liters of saline to wash out the entire small bowel, ensuring all intestinal loops are free. The bowel is moved around with shaking movements to facilitate suction irrigation, effectively washing out any oozing. The suction removes fluid from the pelvis, specifically from the cul-de-sac, with the bladder visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon repeatedly suctions fluid from the abdominal cavity, then leaves three liters of crystalloid solution inside to allow the intestines to float, aiming to prevent further adhesions. The solution is expected to be absorbed over the next two to three days.
The surgeon observes the freed bowel loops, previously adhered, now separated and floating freely. The omentum is visible, and suction is applied to remove residual fluid from the surgical field.

The surgeon performs final suctioning of the abdominal cavity, ensuring the head is elevated to direct fluid towards the pelvis for efficient removal. The cavity is repeatedly cleaned with Ringer's lactate, and once clean, crystallized solution is left in the abdomen.
The surgeon leaves a clean crystallized solution inside the abdominal cavity, ensuring there is no visible blood present in the fluid.

and within two, three days it will get absorbed. You have to tell the patient to start moving just after surgery. The patient will be mobile, then it will be better because it will keep on moving the bubble and chances of re-adhesion will not be there. So this is again changing the bottle another again saline will be used. Ringer or crystallite solution you can use. Repeated irrigation will be done up to deep ring. The fluid should come.
The surgical field shows clear fluid without any residual material. No drain is placed to prevent the removal of this fluid, which is intended to keep the intestines floating and prevent adhesion.
The entire liver and gallbladder are submerged in crystallite solution, visible within the surgical field.
And perforation is a very bad complication and that risk is only for couple of days. So this was just a case of subacute obstruction laparoscopic management. So thank you very much. Have a nice day.
